hero.title: NTU Student Association
hero.descLine2: Defending student rights · Advancing campus democracy

news.sectionSub: Stay current with the latest announcements and activities from the Student Association

about.title: About
about.desc: The NTU Student Association is made up of all NTU students, with departments working together to advance campus affairs.
history: The NTU Student Association was officially founded in August 1988, succeeding the NTU Joint Student Representative Council. Its charter, the “NTUSA Self-Governance Statute,” was ratified by a campus-wide student vote in 1990. Today the Association is in its 38th term.

NTUSA traces its roots to the post-war “Joint Student Self-Governance Council,” formed by the self-governing bodies of each college. After the 1949 “April 6 Incident,” the university tightened its oversight of student self-governing organizations. The structure was reorganized into a “Joint Council of College Representative Assemblies” composed of each college’s representative assembly plus a first-year representative assembly, electing a chair, vice chair, and division heads — the direct predecessor of the Joint Representative Council (JRC) that preceded the Student Association.

Over time the JRC was renamed from the “Joint Council of College Representative Assemblies” to the “Joint Student Representative Council,” and its relationship with each college’s representative assembly shifted from hierarchical to independent. The JRC chair, originally elected by college representatives, came to be elected by class representatives. Starting in 1962, the chair’s term was extended from one semester to one academic year, allowing for sustained initiatives. The JRC of this era enjoyed enormous advantages — staffing and funding exceeding every other campus organization, with each term listing one to two hundred named officers and three to four hundred actual staff. It published four issues a year of the 100-plus-page “NTU Youth” magazine and ran almost every university-wide activity — the JRC’s golden age.

When the Student Activity Center opened in 1971, new student clubs began to proliferate and campus activities diversified. The JRC of this period launched the “NTU Festival” series, the carnival fair, joint academic-club exhibitions, music festivals, departmental exhibitions, and a tutoring center. Riding the wave of the Diaoyu Islands protection movement, the JRC also opened a new round of reflection on democratization and free speech. Then-chair Wang Fu-su passed a motion in the class-representatives’ assembly to rename the “Joint Student Representative Council” to the “Student Union” and to amend the charter to provide for direct election of the chair — only to be vetoed by the administration. The College of Law’s student representative assembly, under successive chairs Hung San-hsiung and Chen Ling-yu, used the law-school journal “Faayan” to push hard against the limits of speech under martial law. By the late 1970s, however, participation in the class-representatives’ assembly had dropped to the point that it often failed to make quorum, and the JRC briefly substituted an advisory council in its place.

Entering the 1980s, the demand for democracy became Taiwan’s shared voice. In late 1982, on the eve of the JRC chair election, a group of five published editorials in student publications including “University News,” “NTU Faayan,” and “Medical News,” calling for direct election of the JRC chair — and stunned the campus. Direct election became NTU’s central political issue for years afterward. In 1985 the student representative assembly passed a direct-election motion 94–0, only to be vetoed by the administration again. In 1986, after the university suspended the campus newspaper society for one year over an editorial-review violation, students launched the “Love of Freedom” movement, calling for academic freedom, freedom of assembly, faculty governance, and university autonomy. That same year the JRC elected Chen Chih-jou as chair; during his term the administration agreed to direct election of the student association president, and Ho Jung-hsing was elected as the first student senate speaker, preparing the JRC for transition into the Student Association.

In 1988, with 53% turnout, NTU students elected Lo Wen-chia as the first president of the Student Association. Debate over whether the Association should adopt a presidential or parliamentary system quickly became the next focal point. After lengthy deliberation through the charter-drafting committee and the student senate, the “NTUSA Self-Governance Statute,” approved by campus-wide referendum in 1990, was officially established as the Association’s foundational law. After several more years of debate, key legislation governing the executive, legislative, judicial, and electoral branches was completed across 1992.

The early terms of NTUSA — riding the wave of the Wild Lily student movement — became a bridgehead for progressive issues. Second-term president Fan Yun, third-term Lin Yi-hua, and sixth-term Huang Kuo-chang all later entered politics. Voter turnout exceeded 30%. During this period, NTUSA pushed the Legislative Yuan to amend the University Act in 1993, establishing institutional protection for university autonomy. In 1994 it pressed the university council to abolish compulsory military and nursing courses. Responding to the University Act amendment, NTU revised its organizational statute across 1993–1994, opening debate around student representation in the university council, NTUSA and the Graduate Student Association’s respective authorities, and the collection of association dues. In 1995 the student court issued Interpretation No. 3, confirming that graduate students are de jure members of NTUSA.

The 1996 eighth-term presidential election was the first whose turnout fell below 20%. The student court issued Interpretation No. 4, ruling that a turnout threshold violates the student charter. As democratization advanced, student clubs operated on equal footing with NTUSA, and students no longer had to channel opinions exclusively through the Association. NTUSA responded by experimenting with new activities — launching the NTU Film Festival, NTU Arts Season, and Azalea Festival — while pursuing campus issues such as the decommissioning of Zhoushan Road and preserving spring break. The 2001 fourteenth-term presidential election remains the only NTUSA presidential election ever invalidated by the student court, over election-administration issues. During 2002–2003 the senate passed the Budget and Final-Accounts Acts, establishing the foundation of the Association’s financial system.

In 2008, under twenty-first-term president Hsu Ching-fang, NTUSA actively joined social movements — engaging with the Wild Strawberries movement and partnering with campus publications such as “Consciousness” and “Island News” to launch the “Hundred Years of Reform” series, challenging the administration’s fixation on entering the global top 100 at the expense of more important issues. Twenty-third-term president Chen Yi-chi’s term was marked by major controversy after a four-school joint charity concert ran a NT$730,000 loss. From 2009 to 2011 the senate progressively revised executive, legislative, judicial, and electoral statutes, established the Election Commission, and passed major self-governance laws including the Dues Collection Act, Presidential Succession Act, Senate Powers Act, and Senate Oversight Act.

Aligned with social trends, NTUSA established new policy departments — the Gender Department (now the Academic Department’s Gender Equality Workshop), External Affairs (now International Affairs), the Sustainability Department (now the Academic Department’s Sustainability Group), and the IT Department — bringing the Association closer to student needs. In 2015 the senate passed the Pluralistic Gender Equality Promotion Act, signaling support for diverse values. The Election Commission piloted electronic voting in 2014, but after years of experimentation and debate, formally abolished it in 2019 over system-stability and secret-ballot concerns.

With the new presidential selection process, NTUSA worked to expand student participation in choosing the university president. Thirtieth-term president Lin Yen-ting, while serving as a student selection committee member, launched “Mr. President, May I Ask?” — bringing candidates face to face with students. In the subsequent controversy over the appointment of incoming NTU president Kuan Chung-ming, student representatives maintained an insistence on procedural justice and spoke out actively in the university council and in the press.

Facing political pressure from inside and outside campus, NTUSA has continued to push issues across campus life — reducing service-learning requirements, abolishing required college Chinese, advancing transitional justice and indigenous-language initiatives, and pushing back on grade-publication news — winning occasional victories. Every inch of progress takes years of effort.

From 2020 to 2022, three years of pandemic shook both Taiwanese society and the NTU campus. While supporting students through the pandemic, reviewing campus pandemic policy, and launching pandemic crowd-editing efforts, the Association also faced existential pressure of its own. Under Level 3 alert restrictions on gatherings, in 2021 NTUSA failed to hold a presidential election on schedule for the first time. Low turnout and declining revenues have become the norm under pandemic and post-pandemic pressures. Even so, NTUSA has continued to push for a car-free campus, launched Open House NTU, and organized roundtables with university-level administrators.

In 2025, NTUSA briefly had a one-minute president. In March 2026, then-president Chen Po-cheng became the first NTUSA president ever recalled. From the Joint Representative Council to today’s Student Association, we have carried NTU students’ journey through democracy — bearing the dream of “shaping a free and equal society, building a democratic and lawful nation.”
about.deptsSub: Multiple departments work together to defend student rights

pr.name: PR Department
pr.desc: Working to improve student welfare through partnerships with vendors and cross-sector collaborations. Beyond organizing participatory events such as the midterm and final exam supply stations, we lead social-media content and information flow, and serve as the Association’s outward-facing point of contact. The goal is to channel external resources back to the campus and improve student welfare.

it.name: IT Department
it.desc: The IT Department is the bridge between the Student Association and the campus digital environment. We advocate to improve campus IT systems students use daily — networking, printers, and more — and track information security and privacy issues, handling complaints and incident reports. On the engineering side, we maintain the finance-reimbursement system, the NTUSA website, and the cross-department data and password infrastructure.

finance.name: Finance Department
finance.desc: Responsible for budget and final-accounts preparation, internal reimbursements, on-campus expense settlement, and overall financial oversight of the Association.

rights.title: Announcements
rights.desc: The latest announcements, statements, and explanations from the Student Association on student rights.

forms.title: Links
forms.desc: Links to department websites, student-rights complaint channels, and external resources.
forms.lineCardDesc: Add @ntusa on LINE to report student-rights issues directly to the Association.
forms.comingSoonDesc: Department links will be added progressively.

data.title: Public Records

campusTools.heroLead: Apps and services built by fellow students to make coursework, daily life, and club operations easier. We focus on “what you get,” not technical specs.
campusTools.categorySub: Small helpers and services that improve campus life
campusTools.submitTitle: Want more people to see your project?
campusTools.submitDesc: If you maintain a website, tool, or LINE service useful to NTU students, please reach out to the NTUSA IT Department; we'll verify the details and publish it here.
campusTools.submitNote: Also watch for other contact channels announced by the Student Association.
